title: Some Neanderthals Were Genetically Healthy Right Up Until the End
gist: A Nature study of DNA from 27 Neanderthals in the Meuse River basin (~50,000 years ago) finds this population was genetically healthy with no signs of inbreeding, challenging inbreeding-driven explanations for Neanderthal extinction.

insights.claim: DNA from 27 Neanderthals in the Meuse River basin (Germany/Belgium) living ~50,000 years ago showed no signs of inbreeding and were genetically healthy, unlike other studied populations, per a new Nature study.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Undercuts inbreeding as a universal extinction cause; basic paleogenetics with no market read.
insights.quote: determined they were genetically healthy (unlike other populations)
insights.locator: An international team of evolutionary anthropologists

insights.claim: Neanderthals thrived for 200,000 years across Central Asia, Europe, and the Middle East before mysteriously vanishing around 40,000 years ago, with climate change, humans, and inbreeding all flagged as candidate causes.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Context for the still-open question of why Neanderthals disappeared.
insights.quote: Around 40,000 years ago they mysteriously vanished.
insights.locator: Neanderthals thrived on Earth for 200,000 years
